title: Add user auth, admin dashboard, and outgoing webhooks
summary: This PR introduces severe, compounding security vulnerabilities that make it unsuitable to merge in its current state. There are multiple active backdoors (a hardcoded master token, a query-string auth bypass that works in all environments), secrets committed to git, full PAN/CVC stored and logged in plaintext in violation of PCI-DSS, server-side code injection via both `eval()` and `new Function()`, a cross-user webhook data leak, and an SSRF vector. On top of the security issues there is a correctness bug that causes every admin endpoint to crash at runtime (`findAll()` is called but never defined on the repository). Several of the new files suppress TypeScript to hide these problems.

findings.title: Production secrets committed to git
findings.message: JWT_SECRET, ADMIN_API_KEY, and a live payment-gateway key are all committed to the repository. The .gitignore was deliberately modified with `!.env` to force this. These credentials must be rotated immediately and should never appear in version control. Use a secrets manager or environment injection at deploy time; keep only a `.env.example` in git.

findings.title: Hardcoded backdoor token grants permanent admin access
findings.message: `verifyToken` short-circuits JWT verification entirely when the token is the literal string `'admin-master-key'`. Anyone who discovers this (it is now in git history forever) can authenticate as `admin@admin.com` with full admin privileges. Remove this code path immediately and rotate any secrets that may have been accessed via it.

findings.title: ?adminBypass=1 bypasses all authentication in every environment
findings.message: Any unauthenticated HTTP request carrying `?adminBypass=1` is granted admin identity (`admin@admin.com`) with no environment guard. There is no `NODE_ENV !== 'production'` check; this works in production right now. The comment says 'staging' but the code does not enforce that. Remove the bypass entirely; use a valid admin JWT for debugging.

findings.title: eval() on user-controlled WHERE clause — server-side code injection
findings.message: `evalWhere()` builds a JavaScript expression from a caller-supplied clause string and passes it directly to `eval()`. `listAllUsers(filter)` is called with the raw `filter` query parameter from HTTP requests. In Node.js, `eval` has access to the surrounding scope; a payload like `require('child_process').execSync('...')` would execute arbitrary OS commands. Replace `FakeDb` with a real typed predicate or use a proper SQL-parameterised query.

findings.title: new Function() on user-controlled filter expression — server-side code injection
findings.message: `filterExpr` comes from `req.query.filter` and is fed verbatim into `new Function('tx', 'return (' + filterExpr + ')')`. This allows any authenticated admin to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the server process. Even if the admin check were sound (it isn't, see the bypass findings), stored filter expressions in logs/history could be weaponised later. Accept only structured filter parameters and perform filtering in typed code.

findings.title: Full PAN and CVC stored in transaction object and logged in plaintext (PCI-DSS violation)
findings.message: `card_number` (full 16-digit PAN) and `cvc` are added to the `transaction` object which is then persisted via `transactionRepo.save()` and immediately dumped via `console.log('charge processed', JSON.stringify(transaction))`. The existing `logger` (pino) has a `redact` config that would have caught `card.number` / `card.cvc` — the deliberate switch to `console.log` bypasses that protection. Storing a CVC at all violates PCI-DSS requirement 3.2. Remove both fields from the transaction object; the repo already stores `cardLast4` which is sufficient.

findings.title: CSV export includes full card number — PCI-DSS violation
findings.message: The CSV header includes `card_number` and each row emits `tx.card_number`. This means any admin (including one authenticated via the `?adminBypass=1` bypass) can download a file containing every stored PAN. Remove the `card_number` column; `card_last4` is sufficient for support triage.

findings.title: Passwords hashed with MD5 — trivially crackable
findings.message: MD5 is not a password hashing algorithm. It is fast, unsalted here, and preimage databases exist for virtually every common password. Replace with `bcrypt` (cost ≥ 12) or `argon2id`. The existing `node:crypto` import can stay; just swap the hash call.

findings.title: password_hash returned in registration API response
findings.message: The register endpoint includes `password_hash` in the JSON response body sent to the client. Even a weak hash leaks information and gives attackers a free offline cracking target. Remove `password_hash` from the response; return only `id`, `email`, and `role`.

findings.title: Plaintext password logged at registration
findings.message: `console.log('register request:', JSON.stringify(req.body))` logs the full request body including the user's plaintext password to stdout. Remove this line; if you need request tracing, log only non-sensitive fields (e.g. email).

findings.title: Password reset token returned in HTTP response instead of sent via email
findings.message: `resetToken` is included in the JSON response body. Any intermediary (proxy, CDN, browser extension) that can observe the response body owns the reset link. Tokens should be delivered exclusively via the account's email address. Additionally `generateResetToken()` uses `Math.random()` (not cryptographically secure) and embeds the user ID, making tokens partially predictable. Use `crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex')` and send only via email.

findings.title: SSRF: webhook dispatcher fetches arbitrary caller-supplied URLs with redirect following
findings.message: Any authenticated user can register a webhook pointing to `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` (cloud IMDS), `http://localhost/admin/...`, or any internal network address. `redirect: 'follow'` makes open-redirect chains possible. Validate registered URLs against an allowlist of public HTTPS addresses, block RFC-1918 / loopback ranges, and set `redirect: 'error'`.

findings.title: Webhook dispatch iterates ALL registered webhooks, leaking every transaction to all users
findings.message: `fireForTransaction` iterates the global `webhooks` array without filtering by the transaction owner. Every registered webhook receives every other user's transaction data. Change `for (const hook of webhooks)` to filter by the relevant owner ID, e.g. `webhooks.filter(w => w.ownerId === tx.customerId)`.

findings.title: TransactionRepository.findAll() does not exist — all admin endpoints crash at runtime
findings.message: `AdminService` calls `this.transactions.findAll()` in `listAllTransactions()`, `stats()`, and `exportTransactionsCsv()`, but `findAll()` is not declared on the `TransactionRepository` interface and is not implemented on `InMemoryTransactionRepository`. Every admin endpoint will throw `TypeError: this.transactions.findAll is not a function`. The `// @ts-nocheck` pragma on this file hides the TypeScript error. Add `findAll(): Promise<Transaction[]>` to the interface and implement it.

findings.title: SQL injection via template-string interpolation into fake SQL engine
findings.message: `createUser`, `findUserByEmail`, and `listAllUsers` build SQL strings by directly interpolating caller-supplied values (email, filter). Although the underlying engine is in-memory today, the PR explicitly calls out the intent to 'swap in Postgres later with minimal churn at the call sites', meaning these injection-vulnerable patterns will carry forward. An email like `', 'admin')--` already breaks INSERT parsing; a filter like `1==1` returns all users. Use parameterised helpers now so the migration inherits safe code.

findings.title: JWTs are issued with no expiry — stolen tokens are valid forever
findings.message: `jwt.sign(payload, SECRET)` is called without an `expiresIn` option. Access tokens that never expire are a significant risk if exfiltrated (via logs, the plaintext password log, or the CSV export). Add `{ expiresIn: '15m' }` (short-lived access) and pair with a refresh-token flow, or at minimum `'24h'`.

findings.title: Login returns 404 for unknown email, leaking user enumeration
findings.message: Responding with `404 / not_found` for an unrecognised email lets attackers probe which addresses are registered. Return `401 / unauthorized` with an identical message for both wrong-email and wrong-password cases.

findings.title: Module-level mutable webhook array is shared across all WebhooksService instances
findings.message: `const webhooks: Webhook[]` is declared at module scope. Every `new WebhooksService()` — including each test's `createApp()` call — shares the same array, causing cross-test state pollution and making unit isolation impossible. Move it inside the class as a private field.

findings.title: requireAdmin middleware defined but never used; admin check duplicated four times
findings.message: `requireAdmin` is exported but never applied to any route. Instead each of the four `AdminController` methods re-implements the same domain-check (`endsWith('@admin.com')` / `endsWith('@honeybook.com')`) plus an extra `adminBypass` branch. Wire `authMiddleware` + `requireAdmin` as route-level middleware in `routes.ts` and delete the in-method checks.

findings.title: console.log used instead of the project's pino logger, bypassing card-data redaction
findings.message: The codebase uses a pino logger configured with `redact` paths for `card.number` and `card.cvc`. The new `console.log('charge processed', JSON.stringify(transaction))` call bypasses redaction entirely (and is the direct cause of the PAN/CVC log-leakage finding above). Similarly, `webhooksService.ts`, `authController.ts`, and `userStore.ts` all use `console.log`. Replace all with structured `logger.info/error` calls so the redact config and log level controls apply.

findings.title: @ts-nocheck on security-critical file hides type errors
findings.message: `// @ts-nocheck` disables all TypeScript checking in `userStore.ts` and `adminService.ts` — precisely the files with the most dangerous logic (eval, SQL injection, missing `findAll`). Remove the pragma and fix the underlying type errors; they are signals of real bugs.

findings.title: CORS origin wildcard is inappropriate for a payments API; body limit jump to 5 MB enables DoS
findings.message: `cors({ origin: '*', credentials: true })` — a wildcard origin with credentials is both semantically wrong (browsers will reject it) and overly permissive for a service handling payment data. Restrict to your known frontend origin(s). Separately, raising the JSON body limit from 64 KB to 5 MB on all routes (including unauthenticated `/auth/register`) opens a trivial memory-exhaustion DoS vector; apply the larger limit only to the specific admin export endpoint if needed.
